Centre (Baixa/Chiado) — expensive, touristy. Alfama/Mouraria — historic, trendy but noisy. Avenidas Novas / Saldanha / Areeiro — modern, metro, family-friendly. Parque das Nações — new, family-oriented. Campo de Ourique / Estrela — intimate, green. Almada / Cacilhas — the other bank, cheaper. Cascais / Oeiras — suburbs by the ocean.
Shopping centre, cafes, museums. Very touristy. Rent for a T1 from €1,500-2,500/month. Pros: everything nearby. Cons: crowds, noise at night.
Oldest neighbourhood, fado, cool streets. Characterful, but limited parking, poor accessibility for families with strollers. T1 from €1,200-2,000/month.
Nightlife (Bairro Alto) and boutiques (Príncipe Real). Noisy at night, not a good choice for sleep if you're not a party-goer. T1 €1,500-2,500.
Business centre. Metro on blue + yellow lines. Many foreigners, restaurants, Corte Inglés. T1 €1,400-2,200, T2 €1,800-3,000.
Quiet, with schools. Green line metro. Choice of Russian-speaking clubs. T1 €1,200-€1,800.
Built for Expo 98. Modern, spacious, many parks, oceanarium. Family-friendly. T1 €1,400-€2,200, T2 €2,000-€3,500.
Charming, green, market. A favourite area for expats. T1 €1,300-€2,000.
Diplomatic, with Estrela garden, prestigious. T1 €1,500-€2,500, villas €3,000+.
City centre of Lisboa, but cheaper. Developing, trendy for young people. T1 from €800-€1,500.
Multicultural, diverse cuisines. Green line metro. T1 €1,000-€1,600.
Northern part, quiet. Schools, parks. T1 €900-€1,400, T2 €1,300-€2,000.
On the southern side of the Tejo. 10 minutes by ferry to the centre of Lisboa. -30-40% off city prices. T1 €600-€1,000.
Even further south. Many commuting workers from Lisbon. T1 €500-€900.
Resort town by the ocean. Prestigious, many expats. Train (Urbanos) to Lisboa 40 minutes. T1 €1,500-€3,000, villas €5,000+.
Between Lisboa and Cascais. Tech hub (Lagoas Park). International schools. T1 €1,200-€2,200.
Between Cascais and Oeiras. Casino, beaches. Expensive. T1 €1,500-€2,800.
Work in the centre + no car → Avenidas Novas, Areeiro, Saldanha
Family with children + schools → Parque das Nações, Oeiras, Cascais, Areeiro
Nightlife → Bairro Alto, Cais do Sodré (but not to live)
The atmosphere of the old town → Alfama, Mouraria, Graça
The ocean is close → Cascais, Estoril, Oeiras, Caxias
Young expats → Penha de França, Campo de Ourique, Arroios
Câmara Municipal (municipality) of Lisbon — guide to districts and freguesias

Lisbon — a hilly city on the Tagus River, UNESCO in Belém, tram 28 as a symbol, fado in Alfama, viewpoints with views, Jerónimos Monastery and Belém Tower — must. Minimum 3 days, ideally 5-7. Lisboa Card offers free transport + discounts on museums.

Western part of Lisbon, 6 km from the centre. Here is the era of the Great Geographical Discoveries: Vasco da Gama set sail from here. Entrance to most attractions is €10-14, Lisboa Card offers free or discounted entry.
🏛️ Mosteiro dos Jerónimos (16th century, UNESCO) — Manueline-Gothic monastery, tomb of Vasco da Gama and Camões. €12, Tue-Sun 10-18, Mon closed
🗼 Torre de Belém (16th century, UNESCO) — a watchtower on the river, a symbol of Lisbon. €8
🚀 Padrão dos Descobrimentos — monument to the discoveries from 1960, viewing platform on the roof €5
🥐 Pastéis de Belém — the original pastry shop for pastéis de nata since 1837. There’s a queue, but it’s worth it
🏛️ Museu Nacional dos Coches — royal carriages from the 16th to 19th centuries, €8
🎨 Museu Coleção Berardo / MAAT — contemporary art, often free
How to get there: tram 15E from Praça do Comércio, train from Cais do Sodré to Belém, Uber/Bolt €8-12.
🟨 Praça do Comércio — the main square by the Tagus, triumphal arch, equestrian statue of José I
🟥 Praça do Rossio — lively central square, D. Maria II theatre, wave-patterned cobblestones
⛪ Elevador de Santa Justa — cast-iron lift from 1902 in the style of Eiffel, viewing €5.30
☕ Café A Brasileira (Chiado) — statue of Fernando Pessoa, a coffee legend
📚 Livraria Bertrand — the oldest bookshop in the world (1732), Guinness
🛍️ Rua Augusta — pedestrian shopping street with a triumphal arch
🏛️ Museu do Dinheiro — free money museum in a historic bank building
🚞 Ascensor da Glória — funicular to Bairro Alto
The district survived the 1755 earthquake — narrow streets, azulejos, fado from every window. Hilly, wear comfortable shoes.
🏰 Castelo de São Jorge — a Moorish castle on the hilltop, the best views. €15
⛪ Sé de Lisboa — 12th-century cathedral, free entry to the main part
🎵 Fado at Clube de Fado / Mesa de Frades / Tasca do Chico — evening concerts €30-60 with dinner
🚋 Tram 28E — historic route through Alfama, Graça, Estrela. Be prepared for queues and pickpockets
🌅 Miradouro de Santa Luzia — classic view with azulejos
🌅 Miradouro da Senhora do Monte — the highest point, panorama of the entire city
🏺 Museu do Fado — the history of the genre, recordings of great performers. €5
🎎 Museu Nacional do Azulejo — former monastery with a collection of tiles. €8
🌃 Bairro Alto — nightlife. Quiet during the day, after 10 PM — bars, live music
🌳 Jardim do Príncipe Real — park with a huge 150-year-old pine, Sunday flea market
🏛️ Basílica da Estrela — baroque basilica from the 18th century, free entry
🌲 Jardim da Estrela — Lisboners' favourite garden for picnics
📚 Museu Nacional de Arte Antiga (MNAA) — the main art museum, €10
🌅 Miradouro de São Pedro de Alcântara — view of the castle and city centre, Glória funicular
🐟 Oceanário de Lisboa — one of the best aquariums in Europe. €25
🎢 Pavilhão do Conhecimento — an interactive science museum for children. €10
🚡 Teleférico — cable car along the river, €4/adult
🏗️ Gare do Oriente — a masterpiece train station by Santiago Calatrava
🛍️ Centro Vasco da Gama — shopping centre
🌉 Ponte Vasco da Gama — 17 km bridge over the Tagus (the second longest in Europe)
Lisboa Card (€22/€36/€43 for 24/48/72 hours) — free transport + 30+ attractions
Viva Viagem — card for single metro/bus trips, better than a ticket
Tickets for Jerónimos and Torre de Belém — combo €18 instead of €20, but book online in advance (time slots)
Tram 28: early morning (before 9:00) or evening — fewer crowds and pickpockets
Water is not free in restaurants, ask for 'água da torneira' (tap water, not always provided)
Swimming in the Tejo is not allowed — it is polluted. Beaches in Cascais / Costa da Caparica
Evening walk: Castelo → Alfama → Tejo waterfront
Walking route through Alfama — the oldest district of Lisbon

A 3 km route taking 2.5–3.5 hours through the heart of medieval Lisbon. Start — Sé de Lisboa, finish — Miradouro da Graça. A leisurely climb through narrow cobbled streets, a castle, 3 viewpoints, a fado museum. Convenient to combine with dinner at one of the tascas with live fado.
The 12th-century cathedral, the oldest church in the city. It has survived three earthquakes. Inside is a cloister with archaeological excavations from the Roman and Visigothic periods.
Built on the site of the birthplace of Saint Anthony of Padua in 1195. There is a crypt in the basement. On 13 June, the city celebrates with a wedding marathon right outside the church.

A viewpoint with an azulejo panel depicting the 'Capture of Lisbon from the Moors' (1147). From here, there is the best view of the tiled roofs of Alfama and the Tagus River. A breeze, benches, and a floral pergola.

50 metres higher is the 'Gates of the Sun'. A statue of Saint Vincent (the city's patron) with a raven. From here, you can see the dome of São Vicente de Fora and Campo de Santa Clara with the Feira da Ladra market on Tuesdays and Saturdays.
A Moorish castle atop a hill. The walls date back to the 11th century, with a restored citadel and 11 towers. Peacocks roam the grounds. A 360° panorama of all of Lisbon.
6. Igreja de São Vicente de Fora

A 16th-century monastery, featuring azulejos depicting fables by La Fontaine. In the pantheon are the tombs of the kings of the Braganza dynasty (1640–1910). You can climb to the roof for a different view of Alfama.
The museum of Portuguese fado — a form of urban romance included in UNESCO's list of intangible heritage. Biographies of singers, old records, and performances.

A final viewing point under the pines with a view of the castle and the river. Nearby is the church of Graça and one of the best tascas in the area, "Damas". In the evening, enjoy the sunset over the Castelo.
👟 Comfortable shoes are essential — the cobbled **calçada portuguesa** is slippery in the rain
🕙 The best times are 09:30–12:00 or after 16:30 to avoid the midday heat
💧 Bring water — cafes are available, but they are rare and expensive
🎒 Watch your pockets in the crowd of tourists at Sé and Castelo
🚇 The starting point is 3 minutes from the metro station Baixa-Chiado (exit "Rua Augusta")
🎟️ Castelo de São Jorge: ticket €20 (2026), book online
🎵 In the evening in Alfama — dozens of tascas with live fado (dinner + wine ~€35–50/person)

Alfama is one of the few neighbourhoods that survived the 1755 earthquake. The name comes from the Arabic "al-hamma" (hot spring). Before the Reconquista, it was a Moorish quarter, later a fishing district. Today, it is a UNESCO-protected historical area with unique architecture, where narrow **becos**, houses with balconies in various colours, and lines of **azulejo** coexist.
The 1755 earthquake — the context of the neighbourhood
Visit Lisboa — What to visit in Alfama
Belém — route of the Age of Discoveries

A 4 km route taking 4 hours with museums and without — from Mosteiro dos Jerónimos through Padrão dos Descobrimentos to Torre de Belém. The Belém area holds monuments from the 'golden age' of navigation: Vasco da Gama, Henry the Navigator, Magellan. Along the way — the legendary pastry shop Pastéis de Belém (since 1837).
The Manueline monastery from the 16th century, UNESCO since 1983. Funded by a tax on spices from India. Here lie the graves of Vasco da Gama and Luís de Camões. The cloister is a gem of the style.
The pastry predecessors of pastéis de nata made from the original recipe of the Jerónimos monks since 1837. The takeaway queue is quick, it's better to have coffee in the room with the azulejo panel.
3. MAAT (Museu de Arte, Arquitetura e Tecnologia)
A contemporary museum under a wavy surfboard roof designed by Amanda Levete. Inside — exhibitions of design and video art, above — a panorama of the Tagus River.

A 52 m monument in the shape of a caravel with 33 figures of pioneers: Henry the Navigator, Magellan, Vasco da Gama, and others. In the square — a marble compass rose (a gift from South Africa, 1960).
A garden with a fountain between Jerónimos and Tejo. 32 coats of arms of old provinces around the fountain. A great place for a picnic in front of Torre de Belém.

A 16th-century fortress and lighthouse, UNESCO World Heritage site. Historically welcomed ships returning from India. A symbol of Lisbon. Inside — a stone rhinoceros (the first European sculpture of this animal).
Optional. The largest collection of royal carriages in the world. Luxurious Baroque pieces from the 17th to 19th centuries with gilding and painting.
How to get there and logistics
🚋 Tram 15 from Praça da Figueira — direct, 20 minutes
🚆 Cascais-line train from Cais do Sodré, exit at 'Belém', 10 minutes
🚶 From the centre on foot along the Tejo — about 1 hour, picturesque
💶 Jerónimos + Torre combo ticket €16 (saving €4) on visitlisboa
⏰ Book online — there can be a queue of up to 1.5 hours at the ticket office
❌ Torre de Belém is closed on Mondays, Jerónimos is too

In the 15th–16th centuries, Portugal was a superpower. Vasco da Gama discovered the sea route to India (1498), Pedro Cabral discovered Brazil (1500), and Magellan circumnavigated the globe (1519–1522). The wealth from spices financed this region.
The Age of Great Geographical Discoveries — overview
UNESCO — Monastery of the Hieronymites and Tower of Belém

Autonomous region, eternal spring +18-24°C all year round. Funchal is the capital. Digital Nomads Village in Ponta do Sol. English is widely spoken. SNS is operational. ZFM (Zona Franca da Madeira) — special regime for **corporations** (IRC 5% instead of 21%, not IRS for individuals!). For individual residents, Madeira's IRS is usually 1–2 percentage points lower than the continental rate. Rent is cheaper than in Lisboa, but has increased in 2024-2025 due to digital nomads.
🌡️ Climate: +18-24°C all year round, mild winter
🌿 Nature: mountains, waterfalls, subtropical plants, ocean
🏝️ Island — but with all amenities + airport with direct flights
💻 Digital Nomads Village in Ponta do Sol — programme hub
💰 Autonomous tax status: 10% IRS in some schemes
🚑 SNS is fully operational, there are private clinics
Island: everything is imported, some products are more expensive
Windy weather, fog in the mountains
Rent has increased due to nomads — T1 €800-€1,500 in Funchal
Less variety — small island, you can quickly 'explore' it
Seasonal airport — fewer flights in winter
For families with children aged 15+ — limited choice of schools and entertainment
Strong cloud cover can sometimes last for weeks on the coast
The centre — touristy, with shops and restaurants. São Martinho — quieter. Lido — hotels + residences. T1 €800-€1,500.
Digital Nomads Village. Less cloud cover. Strong community of digital nomads. T1 €700-€1,200.
Southwest, beach (artificial with sand). Quiet, sunny. T1 €700-€1,100.
Fishing village near Funchal. Authentic, cheaper. T1 €600-€900.
Traditional houses, dramatic nature. Remote from Funchal (one hour by car). A car is essential.
Since 2021, Madeira has been hosting a nomad programme. Free coworking in Ponta do Sol, Slack community, events. To register — fill out the form at digitalnomads.startupmadeira.eu.
Does not replace the D8 visa — this is an integration programme, not legalisation. The D8 visa is obtained separately.
✈️ Funchal airport: 1.5 hours to Lisbon (€50-200), direct flights to the UK/DE/FR
🚐 From the airport to the city: aerobus €5 / taxi €25-30
🚗 A car is advisable — the terrain is challenging, public transport is limited
🚌 Horários do Funchal and SAM — buses within the island
🏥 Hospital Dr. Nélio Mendonça (SNS), private: Hospital Particular da Madeira
🌊 Porto Santo — the second island, 2 hours by ferry, sandy beaches
Natural pools and the best beaches in Portugal

Portugal has over 850 beaches and many piscinas naturais (natural pools in the rocks by the ocean). Top: Azenhas do Mar (Sintra), Cabo Mondego (Figueira), Nossa Senhora da Guia (Cascais), cave beaches Alentejo (Zambujeira). Blue flags 2025: over 370 beaches. Free municipal pools are available in most Junta. The warmest: Algarve (22-24°C summer), Madeira (21-23°C year-round). Colder: Atlantic north (17-20°C).
**Azenhas do Mar** (Sintra) — picturesque pool beneath the village, restaurant above. Free entry, parking is paid
**Praia do Magoito** — more of a beach with a natural-pool section
**Praia dos Coxos** (Ericeira) — surf beach with rock pool areas
**Praia dos Pescadores** (Ericeira) — sheltered cove with piscinas
**Nossa Senhora da Guia** (Cascais) — small tidal pool, quiet
**Viana do Castelo** — Piscinas Praia do Carreço — designed by architect Távora
**Matosinhos (Porto)** — Piscinas de Leça — Siza Vieira architecture, fees €2-5 for entry
**Cabo Mondego (Figueira da Foz)** — cave pools beneath the cliffs
**Zambujeira do Mar** (Alentejo) — piscinas in the rocks north of the beach
**Praia da Oura** (Algarve Albufeira) — tidal pools at low tide
**Praia dos Estudantes** (Lagos) — rock arch + natural pool
**Praia da Marinha** (Carvoeiro) — one of the most beautiful beaches in the world
**Mosteiros (São Miguel)** — rock pools with sunset views
Bandeira Azul — international eco-certification by FEE. In 2025 — 370+ beaches and marinas in Portugal. Criteria: clean water, sanitary facilities, safety, environmental education.
🏖️ **Comfort + family**: Praia de Carcavelos (Lisbon), Praia da Falésia (Algarve), Praia de Tamariz (Estoril)
🏄 **Surfing**: Supertubos (Peniche), Nazaré, Baleal, Praia do Guincho
🌊 **Wild and beautiful**: Praia das Maçãs, Praia Grande do Guincho, Praia de Odeceixe (Alentejo)
💎 **Instagram hotspots**: Praia da Marinha, Benagil (grotto — boat tour), Praia do Camilo (Lagos)
🐚 **Long sandy beaches**: Comporta (Setúbal), Costa Nova (Aveiro), Praia de Melides (Alentejo)
🌲 **Nature / nature reserve**: Zambujeira do Mar, Odemira beaches (SW Alentejo natural park)
💡 **For comfortable swimming without a wetsuit**: July-September south, April-October in Madeira
Many Câmaras Municipais manage public pools — outdoor in summer, indoor year-round.
💰 **Prices**: €3-6 entry for residents; €5-10 for non-residents; subscription €15-30/month
🧴 **A swim cap is mandatory** in indoor pools
👙 **Swim tops for non-residents**: tourists can use them, prices €2-5 more
🗓️ **Best in Lisbon**: Piscinas Olivais, Piscinas Arena, Piscina Moscavide; **Porto**: Piscina do Lima, Piscina do Fluvial
🌊 **Rip currents (correntes marítimas)**: serious danger, especially southwest — Guincho, Supertubos
🦈 **Sharks**: no recorded attacks in PT beaches
🌊 **Storms**: autumn-winter — big waves, do not swim
🏖️ **Sun**: summer sun in PT is very strong — SPF 50 is mandatory from 11:00 to 17:00
💧 **Jellyfish** (alforreca): sometimes in season, lifeguard will notify
🚫 **Nudist**: legal on 21 beaches — sign «praia naturista». Not to be confused with regular ones
🐕 **Dogs**: prohibited from 15 June - 30 September on all blue beaches; there are separate dog beaches
**Praia de Faro, Ilha do Farol** — on the island, ferry from Olhão, fewer tourists
**Praia do Carvalhal** (Comporta-Melides) — 20 km of empty sand, often deserted
**Praia do Arrifana** (Aljezur, Alentejo) — surf spot with a beautiful cove
**Praia da Ursa** (Sintra) — near Cabo da Roca, 10 minutes walk down — the reward — solitary pos pillars
**Praia da Adraga** — the next beach after Ursa, dramatic rocks
**Praia da Aguda** (Gaia) — 20 minutes from Porto, locals prefer it over touristy Matosinhos
**Praia de Carneiro** (Porto) — in the city, but quiet
**Ilha do Pessegueiro** (Porto Covo) — tidal island with ruins of a fortress

Three different faces of central PT: Nazaré — giant surfing waves (up to 30 m), Óbidos — medieval town behind 11th-century walls, Fátima — a global centre of Catholic pilgrimage. All 3 are accessible on one big trip from Lisbon by car (150-200 km loop).
Nazaré — the capital of giant waves
A fishing town on the Atlantic. In autumn and winter — a spot for monster wave surfing: Garrett McNamara's record of 23.8 m (2017), unofficially up to 30 m. Famous Praia do Norte.
🌊 Praia do Norte — giant waves from October to March. Nazaré Tow Surfing Challenge festival
🏰 Forte de São Miguel Arcanjo — 16th-century fortress on the cape, the best view of the waves
🚡 Ascensor da Nazaré — funicular from 1889 from the lower town (Praia) to the upper (Sítio), €1.50
⛪ Santuário de Nossa Senhora da Nazaré — the main church of pilgrimage
🎣 Praia da Nazaré — traditional town beach (not for surfing)
🐟 Fish market in the morning — cod, sardines
👗 Nazaré women — traditionally wear 7 skirts, one on top of the other
🏛️ Museu Dr. Joaquim Manso — local ethnography
🎪 Festa da Nossa Senhora da Nazaré — September, the main festival
How to get there: from Lisbon 1.5 hours by car (A8), Rede Expressos 2 hours €12, by train — no direct service.
A town 85 km from Lisbon, completely surrounded by 12th-century walls. Traditionally a 'wedding gift' from kings to queens. Compact — 2-3 hours is enough.
🏰 Castelo de Óbidos — now Pousada do Castelo (historic hotel)
🏛️ Muralha — medieval walls around the city (you can walk along the top)
⛪ Igreja de Santa Maria — 15th-century church with 17th-century azulejo tiles.
🎨 Livraria de Santiago — bookstore in a former church
🍒 Ginja de Óbidos — cherry liqueur served in chocolate cups (€1)
How to get there: Rede Expressos bus from Campo Grande (Lisboa) 1 hour €8, by car 1 hour A8.
In 1917, the Virgin Mary appeared to three children in Fátima. One of the most important places of Catholic pilgrimage in the world. Annually 5-7 million pilgrims.
⛪ Basílica de Nossa Senhora do Rosário — the main basilica with the tombs of the three child visionaries
⛪ Basílica da Santíssima Trindade — modern basilica from 2007, 8,633 seats
🕯️ Capelinha das Aparições — chapel at the site of the first apparition
🏠 Casas dos Videntes — homes of the families of the children in Aljustrel (2 km from the centre)
🎨 Museu de Cera de Fátima — wax figures of the apparition scenes
🚶 Via Sacra (Stations of the Cross) — 14 stations on the hill
📅 13 May and 13 October — anniversaries of the apparitions, hundreds of thousands of pilgrims
🕯️ Candlelight processions in the evening — impressive even for non-believers
How to get there: Rede Expressos from Sete Rios 1.5h €11, by car via A1 1.5h.
🏛️ Mosteiro de Batalha (UNESCO) — Gothic monastery from the 14th century, 15 km from Fátima
🏛️ Mosteiro de Alcobaça (UNESCO) — Cistercian from the 12th century, tombs of Pedro and Inês, 30 km
🏰 Castelo de Leiria — fortress from the 12th century, 20 km from Fátima
🐋 Grutas da Moeda — caves, 5 km from Fátima
🏖️ São Martinho do Porto — shell-shaped lagoon, 30 km from Nazaré
🏞️ Sobreiro — an open-air museum, 10 km from Óbidos
17:00-19:00 — Nazaré fish port and beach, seafood dinner
🌊 Nazaré — best for waves in winter, but it's cold and windy
🎫 Óbidos — free entry (town), paid — Castelo Pousada and individual museums
🙏 Fátima — on anniversaries (13 May, 13 October) — VERY crowded, book accommodation months in advance
👖 Fátima — conservative clothing in the basilicas (shoulders and knees covered)
🚗 Driving is much more convenient, public transport is slow between cities
💰 Average budget for 2 days: accommodation €60-100, food €30-50/person/day, petrol €30
Areas of Porto — where to live

Baixa / Ribeira — historical centre, touristy. Cedofeita / Boavista — trendy, design. Foz — by the ocean, premium. Bonfim — up-and-coming, affordable. Matosinhos — coastal, seafood. Gaia (other bank) — cheaper, same views. Rentals 30-40% lower than Lisbon.
Centre, S. Bento, Trindade metro. Touristy during the day, quieter at night (less than Lx). T1 €900-€1,400.
UNESCO, view of the Douro. Very touristy, steep streets. Plus: beautiful, minus: noise and limited supermarket coverage. T1 €1,000-€1,800 if you find one.
Artsy district, boutiques, cafés, galleries. Trendy for young expats. T1 €900-€1,400.
Close to the centre, being revitalised. More authentic, local. T1 €700-€1,200.
At the mouth of the Douro, premium. Beaches, restaurants, families. Far from the centre, need a car or Metro Line 1. T1 €1,200-€2,000, villas €2,500+.
Business district. Casa da Música, skyscrapers by PT standards. Good schools. T1 €1,000-€1,700.
Between the centre and Foz. Botanical garden. Green, tranquil. T1 €900-1,500.
Near the main train station. Cheap, developing, but still rough around the edges. T1 €600-900.
University district, youth. Yellow metro line. T1 €700-1,100.
Northern districts, families. Blue/Yellow metro lines. T1 €700-1,100.
South bank of the Douro, famous port cellars. Yellow/Purple metro line via Ponte Luís I. Same views, 20-30% cheaper. T1 €700-1,200.
To the west. Beaches, seafood, fishing port. Blue metro line. T1 €700-1,200.
North of Porto. Airport. Family-friendly, schools. Car recommended. T1 €600-€1,000.
East. Cheap, but connection to the centre by car or bus. T1 €500-€800.
🌆 Porto is more compact, everything within walking distance
🌧️ Porto is rainier and cooler (ocean, north)
✈️ Fewer international flights, but there are direct ones to Europe
🚆 Alfa Pendular to Lisbon in 2.5 hours

Porto — the second city of the country and the birthplace of port wine. UNESCO site (Ribeira), Dom Luís I bridge, Port wine cellars in Vila Nova de Gaia, the legendary Livraria Lello (which inspired Rowling). More compact than Lisbon — 2-3 days is enough. The Porto Card offers free transport + discounts.
🌉 Ponte Dom Luís I — a two-level Eiffel bridge (his student). Walking across the upper level offers the best views of Porto. Free
🏰 Ribeira — a riverside area with colourful houses and restaurants. UNESCO
⛪ Sé do Porto — Romanesque-Gothic cathedral, views from the terrace. €3
🚂 Estação de São Bento — train station with 20,000 azulejos, scenes from the history of Portugal. Free
📚 Livraria Lello — Neo-Gothic bookshop from 1906, inspired Harry Potter. €8 (deducted from the purchase of a book)

Walking route through Porto's historic centre

Route 2.5 km in 3 hours from Clérigos tower through Lello bookshop, Sé cathedral and São Bento station to Ribeira waterfront and Luís I bridge. The historic centre is included in UNESCO. Many descents, stone stairs — footwear matters. At the end — crossing to Vila Nova de Gaia for the wine cellars.
A 76 m bell tower from the 18th century in Baroque style. 225 steps to the viewing platform — the best panorama of Porto. Nearby is the church of the same name with an oval nave (a rarity).
A bookshop from 1906 in neo-Gothic/art nouveau style. The legendary wooden spiral staircase (likely the prototype for Hogwarts, although Rowling denies this). Entry €8 (deducted from book purchase).
Two churches merged into one façade, separated by the narrowest house in Porto (1 m wide). The side wall is completely covered with azulejo panels (1912).
A train station from 1916. The vestibule is lined with 20,000 azulejo tiles: scenes from the history of Portugal, the Battle of Arcos de Valdevez, the entry of King João I into Porto.
A 12th-century cathedral of fortress type. Cloister with 18th-century azulejo, silver altar in the Sacrament chapel. Nearby is a monument to Vimara Peres, the first count of Portucale.
6. Rua das Flores / Igreja da Misericórdia
A pedestrian street with cafes, boutiques, and a flow of tourists. The Misericórdia church — from the 16th century, nearby is a museum with the masterpiece 'Fons Vitae' (by Flemish masters).
A neoclassical stock exchange palace from 1850. Sala do Árabe — an imitation of the Alhambra, 18 years of painting. Tours available in PT/EN/ES/FR.

The waterfront with colourful houses — UNESCO. The Luís I Bridge (1886, Teófilo Seyrig, a student of Eiffel) — walk along the upper deck in Gaia or along the river on the lower one. Views from the bridge are postcard-perfect.
👟 Steep descents from Clérigos to Ribeira — wear non-slip shoes
🎟️ Lello — timed slots, book online in advance
🎟️ Palácio da Bolsa — only with a guide, tours last 30 minutes, €12
🚇 Finish at Ribeira → metro ‘São Bento’ or ‘Jardim do Morro’ (Gaia)
🍷 Optionally: across the bridge in Gaia — wine cellars (Graham's, Sandeman, Taylor's, Cálem)
📸 Sunset from the upper deck of the Luís I Bridge — the best photo of Porto

The city gave its name to the country (‘Portucale’) and still shares an informal ‘capital’ role with Lisbon. In the 19th century — an industrial centre. The historic centre was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1996.
Uber / Bolt / FreeNow / BlaBlaCar in Portugal

Uber and Bolt are the leaders. They operate throughout the country. Bolt is usually 10-20% cheaper. FreeNow is a taxi with a driver (official taxis). Taxi (black and green) — payment by meter, more expensive at the airport. BlaBlaCar offers rides between cities.
Operates in Lisbon, Porto, Algarve, Braga, Coimbra, Aveiro. UberX (standard), Uber Comfort, Uber Black. Often used for airport trips.
Estonian company. Usually 10-20% cheaper than Uber. More cars in major cities. Bolt Category Econômica / Standard / XL.
Taxis (official black and green) via app. Price by meter. Often more predictable cost during peak hours when Uber/Bolt surge.
Flag down on the street, at a rank, or call by phone. Tariff 1 (day) €3.25 starting + €0.47/km. Tariff 2 (night, weekends) +20%. Airport — fixed prices by destination.
Carpooling between cities. Lisboa-Porto can be €15-25 (cheaper than the bus). Lisboa-Madrid is popular. Payment via app, no cash.
Peak hours / surge: up to x2 multiplier
Compare Uber and Bolt at the same time — prices change minute by minute
Uber has Uber Pool (poolshare) — cheaper but slower (not everywhere now)
Avoid taxi traps in tourist areas — they start the meter before you get in or choose a longer route
For long trips, BlaBlaCar is cheaper: Lisboa-Porto from €15 vs €20-40 train
Tipping: 5-10% is customary in PT for Uber/Bolt (can leave in app)
Payment: any international card + MB Way
If the taxi overcharged — remember the number and complain to Autoridade de Mobilidade e Transportes
AMT — the regulator of transport in PT
Sintra — a day trip from Lisbon

Sintra — a UNESCO city with romantic palaces in the mountains, 40 minutes from Lisbon by train. Main must: Palácio da Pena, Castelo dos Mouros, Quinta da Regaleira. Full itinerary — 1 day, active walking in the hills. Arrive early, leave on the last train. Online tickets are a must.
🚆 Train (recommended): Rossio → Sintra, 40 min, €2.45. Schedule every 20-30 min. First at 06:15, last at 23:15
🚗 Car: via A16 / IC19, 35-50 min, parking is difficult in season
🚌 Car tour from Lisbon with a group: €50-90, usually includes Cabo da Roca + Cascais
💡 Tip: arrive before 9:00, otherwise there will be crowds at Pena Palace

A colourful Romantic palace from the 19th century on top of a mountain. A symbol of Sintra. Entrance to the park + palace: €14. Park only: €7.50. Open 9:30-19:00 in summer, until 18:00 in winter. **Online tickets with a time slot are mandatory.**
Stone walls from the 8th to 12th centuries on a neighbouring mountain. 360° views. Combo with Pena: €20.50. Separate: €8. Lots of walking on the walls — not for those afraid of heights.

A mystical estate from the early 20th century with an underground 'initiation well' (Poço Iniciático) — a spiral staircase in the rock. €15. The garden area features Gothic architecture, grottos, and tunnels. **Must-see.**
The city palace of the kings, from the 13th to 18th centuries. Two white conical chimneys — a recognisable silhouette. €10. In the historical centre.
An exotic palace-villa in Eastern style. Less visited. €8. 4 km from the centre — taxi.
A Franciscan monastery hidden in the forest. Ascetic cells in the rock walls. €7. 10 km from the centre.
Train from Rossio. At the exit — bus 434 (€5/day, hop-on-hop-off) or taxi/Uber to Pena (€6-8).
With an online slot, enter inside, tour the palace + gardens (2 hours). Visit the main rooms + panoramic terraces.
Walk down (30 minutes on a forest path) or take the bus. Walk around the walls perimeter.
In the centre of Sintra. Traditional queijadas and travesseiros de Sintra (pastries). Café Piriquita — iconic bakery since 1862.
The main city palace. Quick visit 1 hour.
10 minutes walk from the centre. Initiation well + tunnels + gardens. At least 2 hours.
The westernmost point of Europe, 18 km from Sintra. Bus 403 Scotturb, 40 minutes. Sunset over the Atlantic.
Train to Lisbon. Last Rossio at 23:15.
🎫 **Online tickets** for Pena are **mandatory** (parquesdesintra.pt). During the season, slots can sell out a week in advance
👟 **Comfortable shoes** — there is a lot of walking on hills and uneven cobblestones
🧥 **Sweater**: it can be windy and cool at Pena even in summer
💧 **Water** — there are few cafés at the top, bring a bottle
🎫 **Combo ticket** for Pena + Mouros + Monserrate — €32, saving €8

☀️ **Summer** (June-August) — touristy overcrowded, but the days are long
🌧️ Winter (Dec-Feb) — quiet, often foggy and rainy, but the palaces are open

How to find an accountant (contabilista) in Portugal

For recibos verdes with turnover >€15,000/year (VAT payers) and all legal entities — a certified accountant (TOC / OCC) is mandatory by law. Cost: €50-150/month for self-employed, €200-600/month for small legal entities. Search: OCC.pt (directory), LinkedIn, Russian/English speakers in expat Facebook groups. What they should do: IRS, IVA, Segurança Social, SAF-T, annual declarations, contact with AT. It is recommended to change if there are more than 3 delays or lack of transparency.
< €15,000 turnover/year — **not mandatory**, you can do it yourself through Portal das Finanças (simplified IRS)
> €15,000 turnover — IVA is mandatory → **an accountant is legally required**
> €200,000 turnover — contabilidade organizada (organised accounting) → an accountant is always required
With the IFICI regime — often hired even with low turnover for complex declarations
**All** Lda, SA, Unipessoal — **always** required to have a TOC (Técnico Oficial de Contas) or OCC member
Montly: IES (Informação Empresarial Simplificada) — submitted annually
SAF-T (Standard Audit File for Tax) — monthly to AT
Annual audit — for companies > €500k turnover / > 25 employees
Employee (contract worker) — the employer pays taxes for you
Property as an individual (rental < €7,000/year) — Anexo F in IRS
Freelancer < 15k — simplified regime through the Portal independently
Investment income without active activity — Anexo G, E in IRS
Section «Encontrar Contabilista» — search by city, specialisation
Check registration with everyone you interact with — **do not work with unregistered**
Plot twist: the government requires TOC to report all clients — if an accountant is without registration, they are breaking the law, and their signatures are not valid
**Facebook groups**: "Portugal Americans", "Expats in Lisbon", "Русскоязычные в Португалии" — ask for recommendations
**LinkedIn**: search "TOC English-speaking Portugal" — there is a sub-category for expat-friendly
**Reddit**: r/Portugal, r/portugalexpats — thread with recommendations is updated regularly
**Coworking spaces**: often have partner accountants with discounts for members (Avila Spaces, Cowork Central)
**For IFICI / NHR**: large firms know the ins and outs — PwC, KPMG, Deloitte (expensive: €1,500-5,000/year)
**Digital-first**: JustAccounts, Cegoc, Gurunima — online accounting with English support, €50-150/month
**Local**: there are options in every city — ask at your residential complex
🎯 **Simple recibos verdes (< €15k turnover)**: not mandatory, but for assistance with IRS — €100-200 one-off
🎯 **Recibos verdes with IVA (€15-75k)**: €50-100/month, includes monthly IVA + IRS year-end
💳 **Payment**: usually monthly direct debit or transfer, some — quarterly
✅ Checklist: what you need to do
**IVA declarations** (quarter / month) — by the 20th of the following month
**Segurança Social** reports — if you have employees
**Retenção na fonte** — withholdings from self-employed contractors, suppliers
**Active status** (atividades) — update if CAE / address has changed
Registration of new atividade in AT
Submission of applications to AT (exemptions, complaints)
Help with IFICI or NHR application
✅ **OCC registration** — mandatory. The accountant must be a member of the Ordem
✅ **Written contract** — describes the scope of work, deadlines, costs
✅ **Monthly reports** — good accountants send a summary every month
✅ **Access to Portal AT** — through your CMD / CC, not through their account
✅ **English** or Russian — if you do not speak PT, look for bilingual
❌ **“We optimise, you won't regret it”** without deep details — red flag, potential illegal scheme
❌ **Without registration in OCC** or with cancelled registration — cannot sign, your declarations will be rejected
❌ **Requests a copy of CMD pin** — no, never. You always confirm yourself
❌ **“I won't inform AT about your status”** — you are responsible to AT, the accountant is an ally, but the responsibility is yours
1. Find a new one — through recommendations or via OCC directory
2. Notify the old accountant in writing about the termination of services (usually an email is sufficient)
3. Request the **transfer of documents** — all your declarations, contracts with AT for 4+ years
4. The new accountant registers as your TOC on Portal AT (through you with CMD)
5. Change access in the Portal — remove the old accountant's access
6. Review the first month of the new accountant's work — for a smooth transition
⚠️ Changing in the middle of the year — possible, but more complicated (the new one must accept your historical reporting)
Closing atividade (self-employment) and legal entities in Portugal

Closing recibos verdes (cessação de atividade): art. 33 CIVA — through Portal das Finanças free of charge within 15 days after the last issued receipt. Required: submit final VAT declaration + Modelo 3 IRS (if there is turnover in the year) + notify Segurança Social. Closing Unipessoal / Lda is more complex: dissolução + liquidação through Conservatória Comercial (€200-400) + final accounts. Process takes 6-12 months. If there are debts — bankruptcy (insolvência) through tribunal.

Notify clients of cessation (email, letter) — this is required by good practice

Deadline: by the 20th of the following month

If credit (AT owes you) — receive a refund within 30-60 days

Reabertura of activity — possible at any time through Portal AT
But: if you opened and closed in < 12 months — AT may flag as suspicious (attempt to evade taxes)
It is recommended to keep ≥ 12 months between close and re-open
New opening — as pure newly-registered: again 12 months carência exemption from SS (if not worked with recibos verdes in the previous 3 years)

Much more complicated and expensive than being self-employed. Process of dissolução + liquidação.

Internships and Estágios — programmes and payment

Estágios (internships) — a common starting point for a career in PT. Types: **Estágios profissionais** (government, IEFP, ~€750/month for 9 months) for unemployed / graduates; **Estágios curriculares** (university partner, no mandatory payment); **Estágios de inserção** (for long-term unemployed, up to 2 years, 80% IAS); **Private internships** (from companies, €600-€1,500/month). After the internship — 70% receive a full-time offer. For foreigners, a valid work visa D / EU passport / TPD is required.

Labour rights in Portugal — what workers need to know

Work in Portugal is regulated by the Labour Code (Lei 7/2009). Key points: 40-hour week (max 48 with overtime), 22 working days of leave + 13 public holidays, maternity leave 120-180 days 90-100% paid, dismissal requires justification + compensation (cap 12-20 months' salary), minimum wage €920/month in 2026. Probation period: 90 days (general), 180 (responsible positions), 240 (executive). Unions are active: CGTP-IN and UGT. Work reports to ACT (labour conditions authority).
Standard contract, with no end date
Dismissal by employer: requires justification (despedimento por justa causa, extinction, inadequação) + compensation
Employee dismissal: 30 days' notice (up to 2 years in the company) or 60 days (more)
Only for objective reasons (conjuncture, temporary project)
Dismissal without cause is only possible at the end of the term + compensation of 18-24 days' salary per year
Fixed-term without a set date (until the completion of the project / replacement of someone on maternity leave). Max 6 years. Compensation is the same as termo certo.
Contrato de prestação de serviços / recibos verdes

Self-employed-like. Pay your own taxes through Anexo B. NO employment rights (leave, sick pay, decree). For protection — the contract must reflect the actual independent status; otherwise, 'falso recibo verde', punishable by a fine of up to €6,000.
**Subsídio de férias** (holiday allowance): 1 month's salary in June
**Subsídio de Natal** (13th month): 1 month's salary in December
**Subsídio de alimentação** (meal voucher): not mandatory, but common. €6-9/working day. Up to €6/day is exempt from IRS
**Overtime (horas extras)**: +25% for the first hour, +37.5% afterwards
**Work on weekends/holidays**: +100% extra pay OR compensatory day off
**Deductions**: IRS (progressive scale 11.97-48%), SS 11%. Gross → net difference ~22-35% depending on salary
**Pay slip (recibo de vencimento)**: must be issued monthly, detailing all deductions
**Standard**: 40 hours per week, 8 hours per day
Lunch break: 1-2 hours, minimum 1 hour if the day is >6 hours
Weekly day off: 1 mandatory + 1 additional (usually Saturday + Sunday)
**Remote work (teletrabalho)**: regulated by Lei 83/2021 — written contract, payment for electricity/communication by employer, right to disconnection
+ 3 additional days if ≤ 3 days of absence due to illness in the year
Must be taken within the calendar year (cannot be carried over without agreement)
If dismissed: compensation for unused days
The first 3 days — unpaid (under general rule)
From the 4th day — Subsídio de doença from Segurança Social, 55-75% of average salary
In case of long-term illness (>365 days) — 70%
Medical certificate (baixa) from family doctor or emergency services
**Dispensa** — up to 4 hours for medical appointments without salary reduction
**180 days**: **90%** (from 2023, Lei 13/2023 — if both parents take ≥ 30 days)
Pregnancy: 30+ days mandatory leave before childbirth
Feeding: 2 × 30 minutes of paid breaks per day until one year
Dismissal for just cause (due to employee fault)

The employer must prove serious misconduct: absenteeism, theft, serious conflict, systematic breach of duties. Written notice + right to defence (direito de audiência prévia). If the court finds it unlawful — compensation + reinstatement.
Notice period of 30-75 days (depends on length of service)
Compensation: **14 days' salary for each year of service** (cap of 12 × monthly salary or minimum of 3 × monthly salaries)
Unemployment benefit: after dismissal for non-fault reason
If an employee is absent for more than 10 working days without valid reason — the employer may consider it as auto-dismissal. This removes the right to compensation + unemployment benefit.
Protection: CBA negotiations (collective agreements), legal assistance, strikes
Autoridade para as Condições do Trabalho
ACT checks: working conditions, salaries, overtime, safety, false recibos verdes
For disputes over unjust dismissal, unpaid wages
Free legal aid (apoio judiciário) — if income < IAS × 1.5

Recibos Verdes = digital invoices for the self-employed in Portal das Finanças (tax portal). Used by all freelancers and entrepreneurs. Simplified regime: 75% of turnover is the tax base, 25% is exempt. Social contributions 21.4%. VAT — only if turnover > €15,000/year.
Select CAE (activity code). For IT — 62010 (programming), for consulting — 70220, for design — 74100.
Regime Simplificado — default for turnover < €200,000/year. Simplified base.
Contabilidade Organizada — for larger turnovers or by choice, requires an accountant.
Turnover ≤ €15,000/year — exempt from IVA (Regime de Isenção)
Turnover > €15,000 — IVA registration, rate 23% (or 13%/6% for some services)
In the first year — exemption from contributions. From the 13th month — 21.4% of 70% of the average monthly turnover.
Issue a recibo verde (self-employed electronic receipt)
Cidadão → Recibos e Faturas → Emitir Fatura-Recibo (for services) or Fatura (if registered for IVA).
Name, NIF (Portuguese tax ID), address. If the client is outside the EU — according to reverse charge rules, IVA = 0.
Services included in the invoice. IVA automatically applies if you are in IVA mode.
For Portuguese clients — 11.5%, 16.5%, or 25% is withheld automatically. For foreign clients — 0.

Pension system — Segurança Social, PPR, for retirees

State pension: age 66 years 7 months (2026), full at 40+ years of service. Amount: average ~€530/month (2026), max ~€3,800. **PPR** (Plano Poupança-Reforma) — private pension with tax incentives (deduction up to 20% of contributions in IRS, 0% tax upon retirement). Foreign pensions (RU, US, UK) under IRS — taxed at progressive scale (10% NHR cap for those who had old NHR). For foreigners: international social security agreements (RU, BR, US, UK) count periods of service.
🎂 **Age**: 66 years 7 months (increases each year according to life expectancy formula)
💼 **Early**: -0.5% for each month before full age (penalty); until 60 years if 40+ years — no penalty
🐌 **Long career** (carreira longa, 60 + 46 years of work): no penalty
⏳ **Late retirement** (after full age): +bonus for each extra year
**Minimum** (after 15 years of contributions): €326/month 2026 (increases according to IAS)
**Maximum**: 12× IAS = €6,446/month (but requires max contributions throughout life)
Pensão 14 times a year (12 + holiday subsidy + Christmas)
**3-6 months before retirement age**: apply for SS Direta
Documents: NISS history, employment history, ID
Request simulation on portal — find out estimated amount
Pension starts on the date of reforma; first payment in the following month
No need to 'leave work' — you can work part-time + receive pension
💼 PPR — private pension with tax benefits
Plano Poupança-Reforma — main vehicle for personal retirement saving in PT.
- under 35 years: up to **€400/year** deduction (for €2000 contributions = €400)
✅ **0% capital gains** on retirement withdrawal (if the term is respected)
⚠️ Cap: minimum hold of 5 years until 60 retirement age, otherwise penalty
💡 For optimisation: max contribute according to age cap each year — best tax benefits in PT
**PPR fundo (mutual fund)**: variable returns, 1-3% annual fee
**PPR seguro (insurance product)**: guaranteed return ~2-4%, lower fees but lower potential
**Self-directed PPR (rare in PT)**: ETF or specific stocks — through specialised providers
**Top providers**: Caixa, Millennium BCP, Santander, Patris, Optimize
**Normal**: at retirement age, as lump-sum or monthly annuity
**Early with tax penalty**: before 60 if active investment — 28% capital gains rate
**Special hardship withdrawal**: unemployment > 12 months, severe illness, disability — 8% rate
**Inheritance**: PPR transfers to heirs without inheritance tax (Imposto do Selo 0% for PPR)
PT residents are taxed on a worldwide income basis
Foreign pensions — declared in Anexo H of IRS
**Old NHR (until 2024)**: 10% flat for foreign pensions × 10 years (for those who qualify)
**Double Tax Treaties (DTT)**: PT has with RU, BR, US, UK, FR, DE, ES — usually avoids double tax through credit
Russia DTT (1968 protocol) — pensions from the RF taxed only in the country of residency (i.e. PT) — straightforward situation
US: pensions taxed both, but FTC + treaty article negotiated — consult a specialist
Provide IBAN + proof of residency to your home pension agency
Some prefer SEPA, some — international SWIFT
**Wise / Revolut**: better rates for monthly transfers
RF: transfers via SWIFT after sanctions — limited, need to use non-sanctioned bank
Experience in any EU country counts towards PT pension calculation
EU citizens receive pension based on country of last work
If you worked in 3 countries — each pays pro-rata its part
Apply through PT Segurança Social — they contact other countries
**Russia-PT**: bilateral SS agreement 1995 — experience counts but cumbersome paperwork
**Brazil-PT**: full mutual recognition, working ≥ 1 year in any country counts
**Belarus-PT**: no direct agreement; experience does not count
Process: Segurança Social requests work history from foreign agency, may take 6-18 months
🚇 **Transport**: 50% discount on metro/bus in Lisbon for 65+
⚡ **Social Energy Tariff**: 33% discount on electricity if low income
🏠 **IMI exemption**: for primary residence, owner ≥ 65, low income
💰 **Complemento Solidário para Idosos (CSI)**: top-up to minimum income (€565/month 2026) for pensioners with low income
📞 **Linha do Idoso (760 24 24 24)**: assistance and information for seniors
🏥 **Apoio Domiciliário**: home care services through municipality / Misericórdias
📊 Retirement planning when moving to PT
📅 **5-10 years until retirement**: open PPR, max contribution per cap
📊 **Diversify**: ~50% PPR, 25% global ETFs (through PT broker), 25% real estate
🇪🇺 **If you have an EU pension**: confirm portability to PT
💰 **Income needed for a comfortable retirement in PT**: €1,500-2,000/month for the Lisbon area, €1,200-1,500 for other regions
🏥 **Health**: SNS coverage is adequate; private supplement ~€80-150/month adds comfort
🏠 **Consider buying** in the Algarve / regional — cheaper and more comfortable climate
👨‍👩‍👧 **Reagrupamento for older parent**: children can bring parents if they live in PT (see relevant guide)
⚠️ **Inflation hedging**: PT government pensions are inflation-indexed; PPR — depending on the instrument
📞 **Consult**: financial advisor + accountant (especially for multi-country pension situations)
